title: Iran Closes Strait of Hormuz Again; IRGC Gunboats Fire on Merchant Vessel
detail: Iran formally closed the Strait of Hormuz again on April 18 (Day 50), stating the waterway had 'returned to its previous state' under strict IRGC control. An Iranian military spokesperson declared the closure was in direct response to the continued US naval blockade of Iranian ports, calling the blockade 'acts of piracy and maritime robbery.' Iranian gunboats reportedly fired warning shots at a merchant vessel attempting transit without IRGC clearance, forcing it to reverse course. The closure came less than 24 hours after Iran's Foreign Minister Araghchi had declared the Strait 'completely open,' creating significant confusion among shipping operators. France and the UK announced plans to lead maritime security operations in the Hormuz region.

media.caption: Iran closes Strait of Hormuz again hours after opening it, IRGC fires on merchant vessel

title: Iran Disputes Trump's Uranium Claims; Enrichment Duration Gap Stalls Deal
detail: Iran's Foreign Ministry officially denied President Trump's claim that Tehran had agreed to transfer its enriched uranium stockpile, stating the material would not be moved 'anywhere.' Trump had claimed Iran agreed to hand over its 'nuclear dust,' but Iranian officials rejected this characterization. The key sticking point in nuclear talks remains the duration of uranium enrichment restrictions: the US demands a 20-year freeze; Iran has agreed to only three to five years. The US also asked Iran to remove highly enriched uranium from the country, but Iran only agreed to a 'monitored process of down-blending.' Pakistan's Deputy Prime Minister and FM Ishaq Dar was traveling to regional capitals to try to bridge the remaining gaps ahead of the April 22 ceasefire expiration.

media.caption: Iran disputes Trump claims on uranium transfer as enrichment freeze timeline remains unresolved

title: Next Round of US-Iran Talks Planned; Deal 'More Than 80% Complete' — Pakistan
detail: A new round of US-Iran peace negotiations is under discussion, with sources indicating talks could resume in Islamabad as early as the following week. Pakistan's Foreign Minister Ishaq Dar, part of the mediating trio alongside Egyptian and Turkish diplomats, said the deal was 'more than 80 percent complete' but that 'both sides need to show flexibility.' Trump stated negotiations were going 'very well' with 'no sticking points,' while Iran maintained a more cautious public stance. The ceasefire brokered on April 8 expires on April 22, creating an urgent diplomatic window. Trump and his special envoys Steve Witkoff and Jared Kushner were said to be actively engaged.

media.caption: Pakistan mediators push for second round of US-Iran talks as ceasefire nears expiration

title: Lebanon President: Nation 'No Longer a Pawn' as Ceasefire Holds
detail: Lebanese President Joseph Aoun declared that Lebanon was 'no longer a pawn' in a broader regional conflict as the 10-day ceasefire with Israel held on its first full day. Displaced Lebanese continued returning home, finding severe destruction in southern villages and Beirut's southern suburbs. Lebanon's Health Ministry confirmed the Lebanon death toll had risen to 2,196+ killed since the conflict began, including 130+ children, with over 1 million still displaced. Israeli forces remained deployed south of the Litani River, with the IDF warning residents not to move south of the river. Some residents expressed deep skepticism the ceasefire would hold, citing prior broken agreements.

media.caption: Lebanese civilians return home to destruction as ceasefire holds on Day 50
